narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite the challenges many face in this field, and I want to help you understand the automated decision that led to your application's rejection in a clear way. The admissions model looks at patterns from thousands of past students to predict whether someone is likely to pass the bar exam on their first try, and unfortunately, it estimated a 57% chance that you would not—which crossed the threshold for denial, as the school prioritizes applicants predicted to succeed right away. Your profile includes strong early high school performance in the top decile of your class in Year 1, solid Year 3 grades landing you in the 4th decile, an undergraduate GPA of 2.40, an LSAT score of 25, upper-middle family income, and plans to attend part-time while possibly working. The biggest factor pushing the prediction toward bar failure was your LSAT score, which is notably lower than what's typical for students who pass, making it the most influential concern in the model's eyes. On a more positive note, your Year 3 high school decile rank pulled strongly in the opposite direction, suggesting to the model that you have real academic momentum compared to averages. Your top-decile Year 1 performance nudged things slightly toward failure risk, perhaps because it didn't sustain as strongly later, while your part-time status added a moderate push toward higher failure odds, as most successful students go full-time. Your undergraduate GPA and family income offered smaller boosts toward passing, being a bit below average but not decisive. Overall, compared to typical admitted students—who often have higher LSATs around 37, full-time plans, and stronger GPAs—your LSAT stood out as the key gap, even with your promising high school trajectory. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black woman trailblazer in law; it's just one model's snapshot based on historical data. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score, exploring full-time options if feasible, and strengthening your GPA through post-bac courses or highlighting leadership that shows bar-exam readiness. Many students in similar situations reapply successfully after addressing these areas—keep pushing, you've got this.
